sentences.base_language: I wake up at seven o'clock.
sentences.grammarNote: Time expressions come before the verb in Chinese: 七点钟起床 (at seven o'clock get up). In English you might say 'I get up at seven,' but in Chinese the time always precedes the action.

**刷牙** (shuāyá) = brush teeth: **刷** (shuā, to brush/scrub) + **牙** (yá, tooth). This is a verb-object compound — a very common Chinese word-building pattern. Other examples: 洗脸 (wash face), 理发 (get a haircut: **理** arrange + **发** hair). **首先** (shǒuxiān) = first of all.
Chinese uses the body part as a bare object: 洗脸 (wash face), 刷牙 (brush teeth). Unlike Spanish or French, there is no reflexive pronoun — the verb-object compound says it all.
在 + place before the verb indicates where the action happens: 在家吃 = 'eat at home'. The location phrase always comes before the verb, not after as in English ('I eat breakfast at home').

sentences.base_language: I drink a cup of coffee.
sentences.grammarNote: 杯 (bēi) is the measure word for cups and glasses: 一杯水 (a glass of water), 一杯茶 (a cup of tea). Always: number + measure word + noun.

sentences.base_language: I leave the house at eight.
sentences.grammarNote: **离开** (líkāi) = to leave / depart from: **离** (lí, separate from) + **开** (kāi, open/away). **点** (diǎn) = o'clock: 八点 = 8 o'clock. For half past: 八点半 (8:30). For minutes: 八点十五分 (8:15). Time expressions precede the verb in Chinese.

sentences.base_language: I take the subway to work.
sentences.grammarNote: 坐 (zuò) = 'to ride / travel by' for vehicles: 坐地铁 (take the subway), 坐公交车 (take the bus), 坐飞机 (take a plane). 去 + verb then gives the purpose: 'go to work'.

sentences.base_language: I arrive at the office at nine.
sentences.grammarNote: **到达** (dàodá) = to arrive (formal). The simpler colloquial form is just **到** (dào). **办公室** (bàngōngshì) = office: **办公** (handle official work) + **室** (shì, room). **室** is a suffix for rooms: 卧室 (bedroom), 浴室 (bathroom), 教室 (classroom).
